title: Donauwellen
subtitle: German cherry wave traybake with chocolate and vanilla sponge
excerpt: Donauwellen (Danube waves) is a German traybake with alternating vanilla and chocolate sponge batters, sour cherries pressed in, a layer of vanilla buttercream, and a chocolate glaze on top. When sliced, the batter layers ripple around the cherries in a wave pattern. It is baked in a deep roasting tin and feeds a crowd. The technique requires no special skill, just correct layering.
sourceNotes: Donauwellen is a classic of German home baking, widely made across Germany, Austria, and Switzerland. The name derives from the wave pattern visible on the cut cross-section. Recipes appear in German baking guides from the 1970s onwards, including Lilo Aureden's baking columns in German women's magazines of that era. The sour cherry version is most traditional; tinned morello cherries are the practical standard.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze in portions before adding the chocolate glaze. Glaze after thawing.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The traybake keeps in the fridge for 3 days. Slice just before serving.

content.text: The wave pattern forms because the cherries are pressed into the chocolate batter after it is spread on top of the vanilla layer. The cherries sink through the chocolate into the vanilla and pull the dark batter down with them, creating swirls on the cut face. Do not stir the batters together; lay them in layers and let the cherries do the mixing.

items.prepNote: jarred sour/morello cherries, drained and dried on kitchen paper

content.text: Heat the oven to 170 degrees C fan. Grease and line a 20 × 30 cm deep tin. Beat 250 g softened butter with 200 g sugar until pale. Beat in 4 eggs one at a time. Fold in 300 g flour and 2 tsp baking powder, alternating with 50 ml milk. Stir in 1 tsp vanilla extract. Spread two-thirds of the batter into the tin. Stir 30 g cocoa into the remaining batter until smooth. Spread the chocolate batter evenly over the vanilla layer.
Press in the cherries and bake

Scatter 350 g well-dried sour cherries evenly over the chocolate batter. Press each cherry gently into the batter with a finger until it is just submerged; this creates the wave. Bake for 38 to 42 minutes until a skewer comes out clean. Cool in the tin for 20 minutes, then turn out and cool completely on a rack.

Beat 150 g softened butter with 250 g sifted icing sugar and 0.5 tsp vanilla until smooth and pale. Spread evenly over the cooled sponge. Refrigerate for 30 minutes until the buttercream is firm. Melt 200 g dark chocolate and 40 g butter together over a bain-marie or in short microwave bursts, stirring until smooth. Let it cool to 35 degrees C, then pour over the buttercream and spread to the edges. Refrigerate for 30 minutes until the glaze sets. Slice with a knife dipped in hot water.

items.problem: No wave pattern visible on the cut face
items.solution: The cherries were not pressed deeply enough, or they were too wet. Dry the cherries thoroughly on kitchen paper before pressing. Each cherry should disappear below the chocolate batter surface.
The glaze was too cold when sliced, or the knife was cold. Dip the knife in hot water and wipe it between each slice. The glaze should be at room temperature for clean cuts.

items.problem: Sponge is wet around the cherries
items.solution: The cherries were not dried sufficiently. Jarred sour cherries carry a lot of liquid. Drain and then pat dry on several layers of kitchen paper for at least 10 minutes before pressing into the batter.
